title: Dressians, Tropical Grassmannians, and Their Rays
overall_summary: The paper studies the ordinary Dressian Dr(k,n) as the fan of regular matroid subdivisions of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n), equivalently as the tropical prevariety cut out by the 3-term Plücker relations. Its main structural construction embeds the secondary fan of the product of simplices ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} piecewise-linearly into Dr(k,n), via a map τ defined from tropical maximal minors of an augmented matrix; geometrically this controls a matroid subdivision of ∆(k,n) from the subdivision induced on a vertex figure. The paper develops tight-span and vertex-figure methods for constructing and recognizing rays, especially coarsest matroid subdivisions, and classifies/computes all rays and all cones of Dr(3,8). For the original problem, the most useful content is the concrete combinatorial model of Dressian cones by matroid subdivisions, the tight-span/coarsening technology for detecting rays and obstructions, and the explicit small Dressian data that can be used to test or refute proposed order-reversing symmetries.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n>k>0. For a matrix V∈R^{k×(n−k)}, form the augmented tropical matrix Vbar=(E_k|V), where E_k is the k×k tropical identity matrix with 0 on the diagonal and ∞ off the diagonal. For each k-subset σ⊆[n], define τ_V(σ)=tdet(Vbar_σ), where Vbar_σ is the k×k submatrix with column set σ and tdet(A)=min_{ω∈Sym(k)} sum_i a_{i,ω(i)}. For π∈R^{C([n],k)}, define Φ(π)∈R^{k×(n−k)} by Φ(π)_{ij}=π(([k]−{i})∪{j+k}). Then Φ(τ_V)=V for every V. Hence τ is injective, Φ is surjective onto R^{k×(n−k)}, Φ is linear, and τ is piecewise-linear.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For σ=([k]−{i})∪{j+k}, the submatrix Vbar_σ contains all columns of the tropical identity except the i-th one, together with column j of V. In this submatrix, each identity column has exactly one finite entry, equal to 0, and the only row not covered by those identity columns is row i. Thus every finite permutation contributing to the tropical determinant is forced to use the entry v_{ij} in row i and the added column, and all other chosen entries are 0. Therefore τ_V(σ)=v_{ij}, which is exactly the (i,j)-entry of Φ(τ_V). This proves Φ∘τ=id, giving injectivity of τ and surjectivity of Φ. The map Φ is just coordinate projection, hence linear. Each coordinate of τ is a minimum of finitely many linear forms, namely the terms in a tropical determinant, so τ is piecewise-linear.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3 (Kapranov; Speyer)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let π be a lifting function on the vertices e_σ of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n)=conv{e_σ: σ∈C([n],k)}, and let Σ be the regular subdivision of ∆(k,n) induced by π. The following are equivalent: (i) π is a finite tropical Plücker vector, meaning that for every (k−2)-subset ρ⊆[n] and pairwise distinct i,j,l,m∈[n]−ρ, the minimum of π(ρij)+π(ρlm), π(ρil)+π(ρjm), and π(ρim)+π(ρjl) is attained at least twice; (ii) Σ is a matroid subdivision, meaning every cell is a matroid polytope; (iii) the 1-cells of Σ are precisely edges of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n), equivalently no subdivision edge is a non-edge diagonal of ∆(k,n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this equivalence from Kapranov and Speyer rather than reproving it. The mechanism is local on the octahedral 3-faces of the hypersimplex. Fixing ρ and four elements i,j,l,m gives the six vertices e_{ρij}, e_{ρil}, e_{ρim}, e_{ρjl}, e_{ρjm}, e_{ρlm}, which form a regular octahedron. The three possible ways in which one pair of opposite vertices is separated correspond exactly to the three terms in the 3-term Plücker relation. The condition that the minimum is attained at least twice rules out a forbidden lower edge/diagonal in this octahedron. By the Gel'fand-Goresky-MacPherson-Serganova characterization, a subpolytope of the hypersimplex is a matroid polytope exactly when all of its edges are parallel to hypersimplex edges, i.e. to differences e_i−e_j. Thus the tropical Plücker conditions are equivalent to every cell of the induced subdivision being a matroid polytope, and this is equivalent to the asserted 1-skeleton condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V∈R^{k×(n−k)}. Let Γ be the regular subdivision of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} induced by the height function V on its vertices, and let Σ be the regular matroid subdivision of ∆(k,n) induced by τ_V. Let e_[k] be the vertex of ∆(k,n) corresponding to [k], and let e_[k]^⊥ be the affine hyperplane containing the neighbors of e_[k]. The natural bijection ι from the vertices e_i+e_j of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}, with i∈[k] and j∈[n]−[k], to the neighbors e_{[k]−{i}∪{j}} of e_[k] identifies Γ with the induced subdivision Σ∩e_[k]^⊥ of the vertex figure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The neighbors of e_[k] in ∆(k,n) lie in the affine hyperplane e_[k]^⊥ and form a vertex figure isomorphic to ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}. Since τ_V is a tropical Plücker vector, Proposition 3 implies that Σ is a matroid subdivision and, in particular, has no new 1-dimensional cells other than hypersimplex edges. Therefore intersecting Σ with the vertex-figure hyperplane gives a genuine subdivision of that vertex figure without introducing extra vertices. By Lemma 1, the height assigned by τ_V to the neighbor e_{[k]−{i}∪{j}} is exactly v_{ij}, the height used by V on the corresponding vertex e_i+e_j of the product of simplices. Hence the regular subdivision induced on the vertex figure is precisely ι(Γ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a polytope, P' a subpolytope of P, and Σ a subdivision of P. For a subdivision Σ, let T(Σ) denote the tight-span, i.e. the containment poset of interior cells dualized. Suppose that the tight-spans T(Σ) and T(Σ|P') are isomorphic, where Σ|P'={S∩P': S∈Σ}. Then: (i) if Σ' is any subdivision of P coarsening Σ, the tight-spans T(Σ') and T(Σ'|P') are also isomorphic; (ii) if Σ|P' is a coarsest subdivision of P', then Σ is a coarsest subdivision of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (i), assume for contradiction that after coarsening there is a mismatch between T(Σ') and T(Σ'|P'). Then some full-dimensional cell F' of Σ' has F'∩P' not full-dimensional. Choose a full-dimensional cell F of the finer subdivision Σ with F⊆F'. Since T(Σ) and T(Σ|P') are isomorphic, F∩P' is full-dimensional. But then F∩P'⊆F'∩P' is full-dimensional, contradicting the choice of F'. Thus coarsening preserves the tight-span isomorphism. For (ii), if Σ had a nontrivial coarsening Σ', then by (i) the induced subdivision Σ'|P' would give a coarsening of Σ|P'. Because the tight-spans remain isomorphic, this coarsening is nontrivial, contradicting coarseness of Σ|P'.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a polytope and v a vertex of P. Let N be the set of vertices of P adjacent to v, and set Q=conv(N). Assume dim Q=dim P−1. For any subdivision Γ of Q, there is at most one subdivision Σ of P such that Σ∩Q=Γ and every maximal cell of Σ contains v.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a cell γ of Γ, let C(γ) be the cone from v over γ and let D(γ)=C(γ)∩P. The sets D(γ) stratify P, though a priori they need not form the desired subdivision. Suppose Σ has the required properties. If σ is a maximal cell of Σ, then σ∩Q is a maximal cell γ(σ) of Γ. Since σ contains v, it contains conv(v,γ(σ)). Therefore two distinct maximal cells of Σ cannot have the same γ(σ), because their interiors would overlap along the full-dimensional cone over γ(σ). Also, σ has v as a vertex and has no other vertices on the v-side of Q, so σ⊆C(γ(σ))∩P=D(γ(σ)). Thus maximal cells of Σ inject into maximal cells of Γ with σ⊆D(γ(σ)). Since Σ covers all of P, every such inclusion must be equality and every D(γ) must appear. Hence the subdivision, if it exists, is uniquely determined by Γ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be any subdivision, not necessarily regular, of the product of simplices ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}. Then there exists a subdivision Σ of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n) with the following properties: (i) the subdivision induced by Σ on the vertex figure of e_[k] is the image ι(Γ); (ii) every maximal cell of Σ contains the vertex e_[k]; (iii) the tight-spans T(Γ) and T(Σ) are isomorphic. Moreover, if Γ is regular and induced by a lifting function V∈R^{k×(n−k)}, then Σ is regular and is induced by τ_V. If Γ is not regular, then Σ is not regular. The paper uses this to conclude that each tight-span of a subdivision of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} arises as the tight-span of a matroid subdivision of ∆(k,n), and in the regular case as a regular matroid subdivision.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Translate ∆(k,n) by −e_[k], so e_[k] becomes the origin, and write T=conv{e_σ−e_[k]: σ∈C([n],k)}. Let U be the vertex figure at the origin, identified with ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}. For each cell γ of Γ, define a cell pos(γ)∩T, where pos(γ) is the positive real span of γ in the translated coordinates. Set Σ={pos(γ)∩T: γ∈Γ}. This construction immediately makes every maximal cell contain the origin and gives the prescribed subdivision on U. To verify that Σ is a valid subdivision without extra vertices, the proof analyzes possible zero-dimensional faces: the vertices of U have the form e_i−e_j with j∈[k] and i∈[n]−[k]; hyperplanes spanned by such vertices have equations of the form sum_{r∈A∪B} x_r=0 for suitable nonempty A⊂[k] and B⊂[n]−[k], while faces of T are cut out by coordinate equations x_r∈{0,1,−1}. A zero-dimensional intersection of these types must be a vertex of T. Thus the conical construction gives a legitimate subdivision. For tight-spans, cells satisfy (pos C∩T)∩(pos D∩T)=(pos(C∩D))∩T, and dimensions increase by one: dim(pos C∩T)=dim C+1. Hence maximal cells and their incidence relations correspond, so T(Γ)≅T(Σ). If Γ is regular with lifting function V, the proof defines a height κ on vertices e_σ−e_[k] by a minimum-cost flow formula. Writing A=[k]−([k]∩σ) and B=([k+1,n]∩σ), κ(e_σ−e_[k]) is the minimum of sum_{i,j} w(i,j)Vbar_{ij} over nonnegative flows w from A to B with total displacement e_B−e_A. On the other hand τ_V(σ) is the minimum over bijections α:A→B of sum_{i∈A} Vbar_{i,α(i)}. Since minimum-cost flow problems with integral constraints have integral optima, the fractional flow minimum equals the bijection minimum, so κ=τ_V. Thus the regular subdivision is induced by τ_V. Finally, if Γ were nonregular but Σ regular, restricting the regular height function of Σ to the vertex figure would make Γ regular, a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Theorem 7, if P is a face of Γ and G is the bipartite graph with vertex set [n] and an edge (i,j) whenever e_[k]−e_i+e_j is a vertex of P, then the corresponding face Q of Σ is the matroid polytope of the principal transversal matroid associated with G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is not expanded in the paper, but it follows from the conical construction in Theorem 7. A face of the product of simplices is encoded by a bipartite incidence graph recording which replacements of elements of [k] by elements outside [k] occur. The vertices generated in the corresponding cone over the face are precisely the bases obtainable by a matching/transversal-type selection in this bipartite graph. These bases define the principal transversal matroid of G, giving the matroid polytope Q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every regular subdivision Γ of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} and every vertex v of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n), there exists a regular matroid subdivision Σ of ∆(k,n) such that the subdivision of the vertex figure of v induced by Σ coincides with Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 7 proves the assertion for the special vertex e_[k]. The symmetric group Sym(n) acts transitively on the vertices of ∆(k,n) by permuting coordinates, and the stabilizer of a vertex acts transitively on the corresponding product-of-simplices vertex figure by permuting rows and columns. Applying a coordinate permutation carries the construction at e_[k] to any chosen vertex v.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every matrix V∈R^{k×(n−k)}, the tropical Plücker vector τ_V lies in the tropical Grassmannian Gr(k,n), not merely in the Dressian Dr(k,n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a lift V* of the augmented tropical matrix Vbar=(E_k|V) to a k×n matrix over a Puiseux-series field K, with valuation val(V*_{ij})=Vbar_{ij}. The first k columns lift the tropical identity, so they are linearly independent, and the column span L(V*) is a k-dimensional subspace of K^n. Taking the lift generically avoids cancellation in all k×k determinants, so for every k-subset σ, the valuation of the classical Plücker coordinate p(σ) of L(V*) equals the tropical determinant tdet(Vbar_σ)=τ_V(σ). Speyer's result on tropical linear spaces then identifies the tropicalization of L(V*) with the tropical linear space defined by these valuations, proving τ_V∈Gr(k,n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A sufficient criterion for a tropical Plücker vector π∈Dr(k,n) to lie in Gr(k,n) is the following. For any vertex e_σ of ∆(k,n), form the analogue Φ^σ(π) of the matrix of Plücker coordinates adjacent to e_σ, and form τ^σ_{Φ^σ(π)} using the corresponding coordinate system. If τ^σ_{Φ^σ(π)}=π for some σ, then π∈Gr(k,n). The paper notes that this criterion is not necessary.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If τ^σ_{Φ^σ(π)}=π, then π is in the image of one of the τ maps after relabelling coordinates so that σ plays the role of [k]. Proposition 12 applies to every such relabelled τ image, so π lies in the tropical Grassmannian. Non-necessity is shown by examples in Gr(3,6): the generic tropical plane types EEEE and EEFF(a) are in the tropical Grassmannian but cannot be obtained by this construction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n>k>1, the map τ induces a piecewise-linear embedding of the secondary fan of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} into the Dressian Dr(k,n). The image of τ is contained, as a set, in the tropical Grassmannian Gr(k,n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 7 shows that if two lifting functions V induce different regular subdivisions of the product of simplices, their images τ_V induce different regular matroid subdivisions of the hypersimplex, because the subdivision of the vertex figure at e_[k] recovers the original subdivision. Thus τ embeds the secondary fan piecewise-linearly into the Dressian fan. Lemma 1 supplies injectivity on the underlying vector spaces, while Theorem 7 controls the fan cones. Proposition 12 gives the inclusion of the image in Gr(k,n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V∈R^{k×(n−k)} be a tropically rigid point configuration, meaning that as a lifting function on ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} it induces a coarsest nontrivial subdivision. Let w be any column of V, and let V'=(V|w)∈R^{k×(n+1−k)} be obtained by duplicating that column. Then V' is also tropically rigid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let Γ and Γ' be the subdivisions induced by V and V' on ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1} and ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k}, respectively. If w is the i-th column of V, then each maximal cell C of Γ gives a maximal cell of Γ' by taking the old vertices e_l+e_m in C and adding the new vertices e_l+e_{n+1} exactly for those e_l+e_i in C. Consequently T(Γ) and T(Γ') are isomorphic. The facet of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k} defined by x_{n+1}=0 is naturally ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}, and the induced subdivision on it is Γ. Since Γ is coarsest and the tight-spans agree, Proposition 5(ii) implies Γ' is coarsest. Thus V' is tropically rigid.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 21 (Feichtner-Sturmfels)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid on n elements, and let c(M) be the number of connected components of M, where two elements are equivalent if they lie in a common circuit. Then the dimension of the matroid polytope of M equals n−c(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result. The underlying reason is that each connected component C of M imposes one independent affine equation sum_{i∈C} x_i=rank(M|C) on the base polytope, in addition to the decomposition of M as a direct sum over components. Within a connected component, the edge directions e_i−e_j span a codimension-one subspace. Summing over components gives dimension sum_C (|C|−1)=n−c(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 22
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Σ be a matroid subdivision of ∆(k,n). Then the tight-span T(Σ) has dimension at most k−1. More precisely, if F is an interior cell of Σ corresponding to a loop-free rank-k matroid M on [n], then the codimension of F in ∆(k,n) is c(M)−1, where c(M) is the number of connected components of M, and c(M)≤k. If F has codimension k−1, then M has exactly k connected components C_1,...,C_k and M is the direct product of k rank-1 matroids, so its bases are exactly the sets containing one element from each C_i. If F has codimension 1, then M has two connected components C_1,C_2; F lies in the hyperplane sum_{i∈C_1} x_i=rank(M|C_1)=l, and M is the product of a rank-l matroid and a rank-(k−l) matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: An interior cell corresponds to a rank-k matroid with no loops, because being interior to the hypersimplex means no coordinate is forced to be 0. A loop-free matroid has only nontrivial connected components, and each nontrivial component has rank at least 1. Since the ranks of connected components add to k, the number c(M) of connected components is at most k. By Proposition 21, dim F=n−c(M), while dim ∆(k,n)=n−1, so codim F=c(M)−1≤k−1. Dualizing interior cells gives the dimension bound for the tight-span. If codim F=k−1, then c(M)=k and the k positive component ranks sum to k, so each component has rank 1; bases choose exactly one element from each component. If codim F=1, then c(M)=2, giving the stated decomposition into the restrictions on the two components and the displayed affine hyperplane equation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 23 and hypersimplex split count
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every split of the hypersimplex ∆(k,n), i.e. every regular matroid subdivision with exactly two maximal cells, gives a ray of the Dressian Dr(k,n). For n≥2k, the total number of splits of ∆(k,n) is (k−1)(2^{n−1}−(n+1)) − sum_{i=2}^{k−1} (k−i) binom(n,i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A split has precisely two maximal cells and therefore cannot be coarsened nontrivially except to the trivial subdivision. If it is a regular matroid subdivision, its secondary cone is a one-dimensional cone modulo lineality, hence a ray of Dr(k,n). The enumeration formula is cited from Herrmann-Joswig on splitting polytopes. The paper also notes that the split rays can be generated by the tropical point configurations of Example 15 together with the duplication operation of Proposition 18.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 24 and 25
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V∈R^{k×(n−k)} be a tropically rigid point configuration, i.e. its columns in the tropical torus T^{k−1} induce a coarsest nontrivial regular subdivision of ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}. Then τ_V gives a ray of Dr(k,n), also a ray of Gr(k,n), and the tight-span of the corresponding matroid subdivision equals the tropical complex of V. Consequently, for n≥2k there are at least T(n−k,k) combinatorially distinct rays of Dr(k,n) and Gr(k,n), where T(m,k) is the number of partitions of m into k positive parts.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first assertion follows from Corollary 14 and Theorem 7: a coarsest subdivision of the product maps to a coarsest matroid subdivision of the hypersimplex with the same tight-span, so the image cone is a ray; Proposition 12 places it in Gr(k,n). For the lower bound, use the tropically rigid configuration from Example 16 whose tropical complex is a (k−1)-simplex. Distribute n−2k additional duplicate points among the k vertices of this simplex. Proposition 18 preserves tropical rigidity under each duplication. Up to the Sym(k) action on the k vertices, the resulting configurations are indexed by partitions of n−k into k positive parts, giving at least T(n−k,k) combinatorially distinct rays.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 27
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Σ be a coarsest matroid subdivision of ∆(3,n). Then its tight-span T(Σ) is either a line segment or is pure two-dimensional.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 22, tight-spans of matroid subdivisions of ∆(3,n) have dimension at most 2. If Σ is a split, its tight-span is a line segment. If Σ is not a split, the paper invokes Herrmann's result that for a coarsest subdivision all maximal faces of the tight-span have dimension at least 2. Combining this lower bound with the upper bound from Lemma 22 forces T(Σ) to be pure two-dimensional.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5 local shape observation for two-dimensional tight-span cells
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a matroid subdivision of ∆(3,n), let C be a codimension-2 interior cell and let S be the dual two-dimensional cell in the tight-span. The matroid corresponding to C has three connected components α,β,γ partitioning [n], and its bases are triples with one element from each component. Every codimension-1 cell containing C lies in one of the three hyperplanes H_α, H_β, H_γ associated with these components, so S has at most six sides. In the regular case, the edges of S are parallel in R^n/R(1,...,1) to the vectors sum_{i∈α} e_i, sum_{i∈β} e_i, and sum_{i∈γ} e_i, whose sum is zero; hence S is one of a triangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, pentagon, or hexagon. The paper notes that for any rank k these are the only possible two-dimensional faces in the tight-span of a tropical linear space, citing Speyer.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 22 applied to rank 3 and codimension 2, the corresponding matroid must have three connected components, each of rank 1. Hence its bases choose one element from each component α,β,γ. A neighboring codimension-1 cell is obtained by moving away from C across one of the component hyperplanes; each of the three hyperplanes can contribute at most two sides, one on each side of C, so the dual polygon S has at most six edges. In a regular subdivision, duality identifies edge directions of S with normals to the hyperplanes H_α,H_β,H_γ. In the quotient by the all-ones vector these three normals sum to zero. A convex polygon whose edges use only three directions with this relation can have only the five listed combinatorial shapes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 28 and 29
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the tight-span of a regular matroid subdivision, coarsening the subdivision contracts edges of the tight-span. For a triangular two-dimensional cell, if one edge of the triangle collapses, then all edges of that triangle collapse. For a parallelogram two-dimensional cell, if one edge collapses, then the opposite edge also collapses.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records these as observations after the local shape analysis. Coarsening merges adjacent maximal cells of the subdivision, and in the dual tight-span this is represented by contracting the corresponding edge. For a triangular tight-span cell, the three edge directions come from the three component hyperplanes and are tied by the relation that their primitive direction vectors sum to zero; the local coarsening compatible with the matroid-subdivision structure cannot set just one boundary length to zero, so one collapse forces the whole triangular cell to disappear. For a parallelogram, opposite edges represent the same local direction and length parameter in the dual cell; collapsing one forces the opposite parallel edge to collapse as well. The paper uses these collapse rules to certify that certain two-dimensional tight-spans cannot be nontrivially coarsened.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 30
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The Dressian Dr(3,8) has 15,470 rays, in twelve Sym(8)-orbits. The classes are: four split orbits with sizes 28, 56, 56, and 70; two 3-split orbits with sizes 420 and 560; three orbits whose tight-span is two triangles joined by an edge, with sizes 840, 1,260, and 1,680; two orbits coming from tropically rigid five-point configurations in T^2 with three or four connected triangles, each of size 5,040; and one nonplanar tight-span orbit of size 420, whose tight-span is a pure two-dimensional simplicial complex of three triangles sharing a common edge. For the last orbit, an explicit 0/1 ray of length binom(8,3)=56 takes value 0 on the following 30 triples and value 1 on the remaining 26 triples: 123, 124, 126, 127, 128, 134, 136, 137, 138, 234, 235, 236, 237, 238, 245, 247, 248, 256, 257, 258, 267, 268, 345, 347, 348, 356, 357, 358, 367, 368. These 30 triples form the bases of a matroid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The example combines known classifications and the paper's computations. The four split orbits are counted using the hypersimplex split classification. The two 3-split orbits come from Herrmann's classification of 3-splits. The next five orbits are obtained from the tropically rigid point configurations constructed earlier in the paper, using Corollary 24 to turn them into Dressian rays and Sym(8) orbit calculations for counts. The remaining nonplanar class is detected by the full computation of Dr(3,8); the paper gives an explicit 0/1 representative and its tight-span. The collapse rules of Propositions 28 and 29 are used in the surrounding discussion to show that such tight-span complexes can be coarsest.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 31
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Modulo the natural lineality space convention used throughout the paper, Dr(3,8) is a non-pure, non-simplicial, nine-dimensional polyhedral fan. Its f-vector is (1; 15,470; 642,677; 8,892,898; 57,394,505; 194,258,750; 353,149,650; 324,404,880; 117,594,645; 113,400). Modulo the natural Sym(8)-symmetry, the f-vector is (1; 12; 155; 1,149; 5,013; 12,737; 18,802; 14,727; 4,788; 14). There are 116,962,265 maximal cones: 113,400 of dimension 9 and 116,848,865 of dimension 8. Up to symmetry, there are 4,748 maximal cones: 14 of dimension 9 and 4,734 of dimension 8.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is computational and is implemented in polymake. A cone of Dr(k,n) is specified by choosing, for each 3-term Plücker relation, which two of the three associated linear forms are equal and dominate the third in the paper's cone convention. Each such relation corresponds to an octahedral 3-face of ∆(k,n), and the three choices correspond to the three octahedral splits. Thus a matroid subdivision can be encoded by a sequence saying, for every octahedral face, whether and how it is split. The naive backtracking algorithm iterates through octahedra, adds one of the three split inequalities/equalities, intersects the current cone, and backtracks when the dimension is too small. The improved algorithm exploits the boundary: each facet of ∆(k,n) is a hypersimplex of type ∆(k−1,n) or ∆(k,n−1), and a partial octahedral-split sequence must restrict on each ∆(k,n−1) boundary face to a known matroid subdivision. This prunes the search using the already computed Dr(k,n−1). To compute Dr(3,8), the authors additionally fix some initial Plücker choices to ensure at least one representative per Sym(8)-orbit, then compute orbit representatives and orbit sizes. The f-vector is obtained by computing rays of representative maximal cones, storing all rays by orbit, representing faces by ray-index sets, and then computing orbit representatives of faces in each dimension.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 32
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There are 4,748 distinct combinatorial types of generic tropical planes in T^6.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Generic tropical planes correspond to maximal cones of Dr(3,8) up to the Sym(8) action. Theorem 31 computes exactly 4,748 maximal-cone orbits, giving the stated number of combinatorial types.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 33
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: All rays of Dr(3,8) are rays of the tropical Grassmannian Gr(3,8).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 12, all ray orbits arising from tropically rigid point configurations lie in the tropical Grassmannian. This covers eleven of the twelve ray orbits described in Example 30. For the remaining nonplanar ray, the authors perform an explicit Macaulay2 computation: they compute the initial ideal of the Plücker ideal with respect to the 0/1 weight vector representing that ray and verify that the initial ideal contains no monomial. The computation is done over the polynomial ring with integer coefficients; the integral Gröbner basis of the initial ideal has only coefficients ±1, so the conclusion holds in arbitrary characteristic.

proof_techniques.name: Dressian as regular matroid subdivisions of the hypersimplex
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the equivalence between finite tropical Plücker vectors, regular matroid subdivisions of ∆(k,n), and subdivisions whose 1-cells are hypersimplex edges. This turns questions about tropical linear spaces into finite polyhedral/combinatorial questions about cells, coarsenings, octahedral 3-faces, and matroid polytopes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a poset question about tropical linear spaces, this dictionary suggests replacing inclusion/order questions by an explicit combinatorial model whenever the relevant relative Dressian has a matroid-subdivision description. It also gives computable invariants, such as cone dimension, coarsening structure, and tight-span type, that an order-reversing involution would have to preserve or exchange appropriately.

proof_techniques.name: Vertex-figure embedding from products of simplices
proof_techniques.description: A central construction fixes a vertex e_[k] of ∆(k,n), identifies its vertex figure with ∆_{k−1}×∆_{n−k−1}, and cones any subdivision Γ of this product into a subdivision Σ of the hypersimplex whose maximal cells all contain e_[k]. In the regular case, the construction is realized by the explicit tropical-minor map τ from a matrix V to a Plücker vector τ_V. The subdivision on the vertex figure recovers Γ, and the tight-span is preserved.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust way to build controlled families inside a Dressian. In the original problem, one could try to construct candidate counterexamples by embedding secondary-fan pieces into a relative Dressian and checking whether any proposed order-reversing involution can act compatibly on these embedded subposets.

proof_techniques.name: Tight-span control and coarseness transfer
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats the tight-span as the dual containment poset of interior cells of a subdivision. Proposition 5 shows that if a subpolytope sees the same tight-span as the whole subdivision, then coarseness of the induced subdivision on the subpolytope forces coarseness of the whole subdivision. Theorem 7 then uses this to transfer tight-spans from product-of-simplices subdivisions to hypersimplex matroid subdivisions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Tight-spans provide small combinatorial certificates for rays and for non-coarsenability. If an order-reversing involution on a Dressian would have to send certain minimal or maximal elements to impossible tight-span types, this technique can expose the obstruction.

proof_techniques.name: Uniqueness from a prescribed vertex figure
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 6 proves that if every maximal cell of a subdivision contains a vertex v, then the subdivision is uniquely determined by its restriction to the vertex figure at v, provided the convex hull of the neighbors is codimension one. This uniqueness underlies the embedding theorem and prevents ambiguity in lifting local subdivisions to hypersimplex subdivisions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When studying extensions of a flat-lattice involution, one may need to know whether local data around an embedded flat determines a tropical linear space. This result gives a template: impose that all maximal cells contain a distinguished vertex/flat and then compare the induced vertex-figure subdivision.

proof_techniques.name: Tropical rigidity as a ray-construction principle
proof_techniques.description: A point configuration V in a tropical torus is called tropically rigid when its lifting subdivision of a product of simplices is coarsest. Through τ, such a configuration yields a ray of Dr(k,n), and Proposition 18 shows that duplicating points preserves tropical rigidity. This produces large explicit families of Dressian rays with controlled tight-spans.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Rays are extreme elements of the fan structure and often easiest places to test whether a global order-reversing map can exist. The duplication method can produce families with asymmetric multiplicity data that may be hard for a proposed involution to match.

proof_techniques.name: Connected-component dimension bounds for matroid cells
proof_techniques.description: Using the formula dim P(M)=n−c(M), the paper bounds tight-span dimensions by k−1 for subdivisions of ∆(k,n). It also identifies the matroid structure of codimension-one and maximal-codimension interior cells in terms of direct sums over connected components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Such dimension and component-count restrictions give invariants of cells/tropical spaces. In a relative Dressian, analogous component bounds could constrain what an order-reversing involution can do to strata corresponding to contractions, restrictions, or flat-lattice elements.

proof_techniques.name: Local polygon-shape and edge-collapse rules
proof_techniques.description: For rank 3, the dual two-dimensional tight-span cells have only a few possible shapes: triangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, pentagon, or hexagon. The paper uses collapse rules for triangles and parallelograms to show that certain tight-spans cannot be nontrivially coarsened, hence define rays.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: These local rules are useful for proving extremality without full computation. Similar local collapse constraints may produce combinatorial invariants incompatible with a desired order-reversing symmetry.

proof_techniques.name: Octahedral-split encoding of Dressian cones
proof_techniques.description: Every 3-term Plücker relation corresponds to an octahedral 3-face of the hypersimplex, and choosing a cone of the Dressian amounts to choosing compatible octahedral splits. The paper encodes subdivisions as sequences of octahedral split choices and uses boundary restrictions to known smaller Dressians for efficient backtracking.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a concrete computational route for small cases of any Dressian-like object governed by Plücker-type relations. It can be adapted to search for or rule out poset anti-automorphisms by enumerating cones/elements and checking order relations.

proof_techniques.name: Symmetry orbit reduction
proof_techniques.description: The computations exploit the Sym(n)-action on ∆(k,n), Dr(k,n), and vertex figures. Rather than enumerating all objects naively, the paper computes representatives and orbit sizes, then reconstructs f-vectors and face data modulo symmetry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matroid M has automorphisms or a flat-lattice involution, orbit reduction can make it feasible to test whether the involution extends to larger Dressian strata, and to identify invariants distinguishing possible images.

proof_techniques.name: Realizability check by monomial-free initial ideals
proof_techniques.description: To verify that a Dressian ray lies in the tropical Grassmannian, the paper computes the initial ideal of the Plücker ideal with respect to the ray weight vector and checks that it contains no monomial. Performing the computation over Z and observing only coefficients ±1 gives characteristic-independent validity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an attempted involution is expected to interact with realizable versus non-realizable tropical linear spaces, initial-ideal tests provide a way to distinguish those subsets in explicit examples.

other_useful_info: The map τ is not linear; Remark 2 gives an explicit k=2,n=4 example where τ_{V+W} differs from τ_V+τ_W. The τ construction is not surjective: Example 10 explains that for k=2 it produces caterpillar trees from tropical line segments, whereas Dr(2,n)=Gr(2,n) contains all phylogenetic tree types; for n=6 the snowflake tree is not obtained from τ. Example 20 records that Dr(2,5), as a spherical one-dimensional complex, is the Petersen graph, also the graph complement of the vertex-edge graph of ∆(2,5), and contains the embedded hexagon coming from the secondary fan of ∆_1×∆_2. Example 11 states that for Gr(3,6)=Dr(3,6) there are seven combinatorial types of finest matroid subdivisions; five arise from τ applied to configurations of three points in T^2, while the types EEEE and EEFF(a) do not. The paper's open questions include whether all coarsest matroid subdivisions of ∆(3,n) with planar tight-spans come from tropical point configurations, what the maximal spread of a ray of Dr(k,n) is, whether all rays of Dr(k,n) are always rays of Gr(k,n), and computational feasibility questions for Gr(3,8) and Dr(4,8). The authors provide computed Dr(3,8) data at http://svenherrmann.net/DR38/dr38.html. Cited background especially relevant for porting arguments includes Speyer, Tropical linear spaces; Speyer-Sturmfels, The tropical Grassmannian; Feichtner-Sturmfels, Matroid polytopes, nested sets and Bergman fans; Herrmann-Joswig, Splitting polytopes; and Herrmann, On the facets of the secondary polytope.
why_relevant: Studies Dressians as parameter spaces of tropical linear spaces and develops their secondary-fan structure. Its ray and subdivision methods are useful for constructing explicit examples or counterexamples to poset-duality claims.
